titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery to John Edward Fowler Sclater, 3 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter was written by Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery (1844-1924). Montgomery was a lawyer and politician. Originally a Liberal and a strong supporter of Gladstone, Montgomery was also a firm Unionist, but by 1916 he believed that Ulster Unionists had no choice but to accept Lloyd George's proposal for a six-county Northern Ireland. The letter is addressed to John Edward Fowler Sclater (1862-1918), a land agent in County Down. The letter concerns a letter, previously written by Montgomery, that Montgomery's associates have urged him to send into the 'Times'. Montgomery has decided to keep the letter private but advises anyone likely to be involved in the 'Lloyd George conversations' (talks to reach a settlement on the Irish question) to read it.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy 3rd June,1916. My dear Sclater , I wrote the enclosed letter about ten days ago
more to blow off steam than anything else. One or two
leading Unionists in Dublin were very anxious I should get
it into the "Times". I came to the conclusion, however,
that as the game now stands it would not be good tactics
to publish it. I was, however, strongly advised to press
the contents upon anyone likely to be engaged in the Lloyd
George conversations. I have sent, copies to Carson and
Long and one or two to Bates. The letter may perhaps
serve as a starting point for any discussions we may have
on Tuesday. Yours very truly
